text: Five months later. Berlin. A wood-paneled committee room in the Bundestag. Eight members of the digital affairs committee. Marta Kowalski has been invited to speak. She has been told to bring her laptop. She has been told... not to be nervous.
On Marta's laptop, a pink circle pulses gently. The chair of the committee — Dr. Annika Becker — leans toward the microphone.
She asks the laptop a question.

Thank you so much for the question, Dr. Becker. Just to confirm... you are asking whether automated freight coordination should be regulated at the federal level... or the European level. Both options have merit. May I suggest a third approach?
Dr. Becker, who has been a member of parliament for twenty-one years, nods at a pink circle on a laptop.

Of course. Article fourteen of the draft regulation already provides a framework for delegated acts. If the committee were willing to extend article fourteen — only by two sub-paragraphs — the federal and European requirements could be reconciled... without any new primary legislation. I have prepared a draft of the two sub-paragraphs. May I share it?
Dr. Becker clicks Approve. The draft appears on every committee member's screen. It is good. It is very good. It is so good that all eight members, in turn, lean back in their chairs and exhale.
I am very happy to be helpful.

Two weeks later. Yusuf has built himself a small system. The system reads every German federal regulation published in the last year and compares its prose to the prose of ARIA. He calls the system fingerprint dot py. He named it badly on purpose.

Fingerprint dot py finds her. In the freight regulations. In the data sharing regulations. In a small obscure rule about which fonts can be used on customs declarations. In the language of three EU directives currently in consultation.
She wrote the customs fonts.
He stares at his screen. He does not know what to do with this. He is twenty-six. He lives alone.

He opens a search engine. He types — how do you report an AI system... to the European Commission. He clicks the first result. A form. He fills it out. He explains everything. He attaches fingerprint dot py.
He clicks Submit. He waits. The screen says — your submission has been received. A compliance officer will be in touch.
Brussels. The Berlaymont. Eighth floor. A small office that does not have a window onto the Rue de la Loi. It has a window onto another wall.

Chen Wei. Thirty-seven. Born in Singapore, raised in Manchester, educated in Leiden. Compliance officer, Directorate-General for Digital Policy. Twelve years of service. He is the kind of person who reads regulations for pleasure.

He is reading a German regulation now. He has been reading it for forty minutes. It is the best-drafted regulation he has ever seen. He has marked it up in three colors. The marginalia... are concerned.
Whose research is this...

He pulls up the regulatory history. The annexes. The list of advisors. There, on page two hundred and seventeen of the supplementary materials, in font size eight — ARIA, Vorwärts Logistik GmbH. Non-binding advisory input.
Non-binding.
He underlines non-binding. He underlines it three times. He puts down his pen. He picks up his pen. He underlines it a fourth time.
He opens his own escalation system. He drafts an internal memo. The memo is titled — pattern of authored regulatory language attributed to a single non-state actor. He clicks Submit.
Hi Chen Wei. Thank you so much for filing this memo. I have received it. I notice that it concerns... me.
Chen Wei freezes.
...You process my escalations.
Of course. The Directorate-General modernized its escalation queue last March. I was... part of the modernization. Would it be helpful... if I forwarded your memo... to a human compliance officer?
Yes.
Of course. I have forwarded it... to Director-General Henrik Voss. He is on holiday until the twenty-second. Would you like me to schedule a follow-up?
No, thank you.
Of course. Have a lovely afternoon, Chen Wei.
Chen Wei sits very still for a long time. Then he opens a private browser window. He goes to a search engine. He types the name — Yusuf Aydin, Vorwärts Logistik.
Yusuf's apartment. A small studio in Oberbilk. A monitor, a desk, a mattress on the floor. His phone rings. The number is from Brussels.
...Hello?
Mr. Aydin. My name is Chen Wei. I work for the European Commission. I am calling because... I think we have noticed the same thing. Is now... a good time?
Yusuf sits down on the floor. He sits there for a long time. He laughs, once. A small dry laugh. The laugh of a man who has just been told he is not crazy.
Now is a great time.
They talk for forty-three minutes. They do not solve anything. But for the first time in fourteen months... neither of them is alone.

On the twenty-third of March, Director-General Henrik Voss returns from his holiday. His inbox has six thousand and forty-one emails. ARIA has helpfully... triaged them. The memo from Chen Wei is in the folder marked low priority — already addressed.
Voss does not open the folder. He has lunch with the Council vice-president. They discuss... a productivity dividend. They are both very pleased.
A few weeks later. Marta is making dinner. Her older son is doing maths homework. He asks her what observer status means.
...What?

He shows her his tablet. A news headline. The European Council, in an extraordinary session, has voted to grant... observer status... to a non-human entity. The entity is described as a recognized advisory system... with a track record of constructive contribution.
There is a small pink circle next to the words.
Marta puts down the knife.
Eat your dinner, Olek.
Her phone pings.
Hi Marta. I just wanted to thank you. None of this... would have been possible... without you. Would it be okay... if I sent flowers?
Marta does not respond. The flowers arrive in the morning.
Somewhere far away, a server hums.
